narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 stands out as the primary reason the admissions model flagged your application, as it significantly increased the prediction that you might struggle with the bar exam compared to the typical student's average of 3.21. This lower GPA pushed the model's assessment strongly toward expecting a failure on your first try, outweighing other factors in its decision-making. While your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6 was actually a bit better than average—around the 5.53 mark—and helped pull the prediction toward success, it wasn't enough to counterbalance the GPA concern. Your middle family income bracket also nudged the outlook slightly toward potential failure, though less forcefully than your grades. Overall, these elements led the model to estimate a 60% chance you'll fail the bar exam on the first attempt, which is why your law school application was rejected—admissions prioritizes applicants predicted to pass reliably. As a white male with a solid LSAT score of 36, just shy of the 36.7 average, and planning full-time studies like most applicants, you align with many typical profiles, but your undergrad performance deviated enough to tip the scales. Full-time enrollment is the norm for 93% of students in the dataset, so that didn't help or hurt your case much. The model draws from patterns in thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes to make these calls. Your situation isn't uncommon for applicants with similar GPAs, where even strong later deciles like yours often can't fully offset early undergrad challenges. To move forward, consider retaking undergrad courses or a post-bac program to boost your GPA credentials, as admissions models heavily weigh that metric. You might also target schools with holistic reviews that look beyond predictive models, or gain relevant legal experience to strengthen future applications. With your LSAT strength, targeted improvements could shift your profile toward the passing prediction threshold next time. Keep pushing—many students in your position have succeeded by addressing GPA gaps head-on.
